Title: Modification of chrysanthemum odour and taste with chrysanthemol synthase induces strong dual resistance against cotton aphids

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the two-step conversion of dimethylallyl diphosphate (DMAPP) via chrysanthemyl diphosphate (CPP) to chrysanthemol.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: First committed step in pyrethrin/chrysanthemol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The TcCHS allele used here includes a native plastid targeting signal and differs from an earlier reported TcCHS clone (I13995) with an incorrectly spliced targeting signal. Chrysanthemol glycoside accumulation in transgenic chrysanthemum is inferred to be produced by endogenous glycosylation/malonylation enzymes, not TcCHS itself.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In transgenic Chrysanthemum morifolium leaves and ovaries, TcCHS transcripts were strongly elevated; expression was much higher in leaves than ovaries in lines CHS3 and CHS11.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Monoterpene synthase; chrysanthemyl diphosphate/chrysanthemol synthase-type terpene synthase
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No significant detrimental phenotypic effects detected in transgenic chrysanthemum for plant height, dry weight, leaf size, chlorophyll, or carotenoid contents.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes emission of volatile trans-chrysanthemol and accumulation of chrysanthemyl-6-O-malonyl-β-D-glucopyranoside, resulting in strong aphid repellence/deterrence and reduced aphid reproduction.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Chrysanthemol acts as an aphid-repellent volatile; the glycosylated derivative acts as a nonvolatile feeding deterrent, together conferring pest resistance.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves; volatile emitted from aerial tissues/headspace, glycoside accumulated in leaf tissue, likely vacuolar storage
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of TcCHS in chrysanthemum generated a dominant new volatile peak identified as trans-chrysanthemol and a minor new volatile trans-chrysanthemyl acetate. 2. TcCHS expression also caused accumulation of a novel nonvolatile derivative identified as chrysanthemyl-6-O-malonyl-β-D-glucopyranoside at about 1.08 mM in leaves. 3. TcCHS transgenic plants showed 2.4–2.6-fold lower aphid population development in no-choice assays and 93%–96% inhibition under greenhouse natural infestation. 4. Pure chrysanthemol repelled aphids, while the purified glycoside deterred aphid feeding, demonstrating dual bioactivity of TcCHS-induced products. 5. TcCHS expression caused no measurable growth or pigment penalty in chrysanthemum.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Greenhouse growth; dynamic headspace volatile collection; aphid bioassays; semi-open greenhouse natural aphid infestation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in Chrysanthemum morifolium; qRT-PCR; GC-MS volatile profiling; LC-MS and NMR metabolite identification; aphid dual-choice and no-choice assays; EPG feeding analysis; greenhouse infestation trial
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Chrysanthemum RbcS1 promoter, strong constitutive aboveground promoter
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Chrysanthemol/pyrethrin precursor branch of monoterpene metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Normally exclusively expressed in trichome cells in pyrethrum
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising transgene for engineering aphid resistance in chrysanthemum and potentially other crops by introducing dual volatile and nonvolatile defense without obvious growth penalty; useful for pest-control trait deployment in ornamental breeding.
